sources.name: Wikipedia — Tiny Toon Adventures (NES)
sources.notes: Character abilities confirmed. Buster primary, sidekick system documented.

notes: Tiny Toon Adventures NES is a Konami platformer based on the animated series.
PRIMARY CHARACTER: Buster Bunny. Sidekick characters have unique abilities and serve as rescue objectives.
SIDEKICK SYSTEM: Three friends can be found in levels — each provides a one-use special ability when selected.
Plucky Duck: short flight/glide. Hamton: can break certain floors/blocks. Dizzy Devil: spin attack through obstacles.
Standard Konami NES physics — similar feel to Contra and other Konami NES titles.
Montana Max is the main antagonist across 4 worlds.

buster_bunny._note: Primary player character. Standard platformer physics.

walk_speed.notes: Konami NES action game movement. VERIFY.

carrot_throw.description: Buster can throw carrots as projectiles.

sidekick_system._note: Three sidekicks are findable in levels. Each provides a special ability when selected.

plucky_duck.description: Plucky flaps his wings to fly or glide for a short duration.
notes: Allows reaching elevated platforms Buster can't reach with standard jump.
Flight duration is limited — not infinite hover.
Similar to Peach's float in SMB2 but character-specific.

hamton_pig.description: Hamton's weight allows breaking through certain floor tiles and blocks.
notes: Access to underground areas inaccessible to lighter characters.

dizzy_devil.description: Dizzy spins forward, breaking through enemies and certain obstacles.
notes: Offensive tool — the spin can destroy things Buster and other characters can't.
Distinct spin animation — the tornado spin from the animated series.
Sidekicks are found as rescue objectives in levels — find them first, then they're available.
Pressing select (or specific button) switches to a sidekick — they have limited use before returning control to Buster.
VERIFY exact switch mechanic and usage limits.
Each world has a boss fight.
Montana Max is the recurring villain across all worlds.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: Tiny Toon Adventures' sidekick system creates a resource puzzle: find the sidekick, use their ability at the right moment, move on. The ability isn't always available — it requires exploration (find the character) and timing (use before losing). This is resource management built around a rescue narrative.

Three sidekicks with three categorically different abilities (flight, ground break, spin) cover the full range of platformer traversal solutions: vertical (Plucky), horizontal/destructive (Dizzy), and vertical-downward/environmental (Hamton). Between them, they solve every obstacle type — the challenge is having the right one at the right time.

The animated series connection gives players instant character familiarity before gameplay starts. Plucky's laziness (flight as the 'lazy' solution to jumping), Hamton's size (breaking floors), and Dizzy's spinning mania are personality-to-mechanic translations. Good character-mechanics alignment.

Montana Max as the villain creates clear motivation: a rich, mean kid who kidnaps your friends. This is an accessible relational premise — the villainy is personal, not global. The stakes are friendship, not world-saving. For a licensed kids' game, this is the appropriate dramatic scale.

The Konami NES formula (tight physics, character variety, staged world structure) was applied to many licensed properties. Tiny Toon Adventures, like DuckTales and Chip 'n Dale (Capcom), demonstrates that the right physics engine can make licensed content feel genuine rather than exploitative.
